questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
questions.hint: Think about how arrays are stored in memory — contiguous blocks.
questions.solution: O(1) — Arrays allow direct index-based access. Since memory is contiguous, the address of any element can be computed instantly as: base_address + (index × element_size).

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of an array?
questions.hint: You need to shift all existing elements to make room.
questions.solution: O(n) — Inserting at the beginning requires shifting all n elements one position to the right.

questions.question: Which data structure uses LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle?
questions.hint: Think of a pile of plates — you always take from the top.
questions.solution: Stack — The last element pushed is the first one popped. Example: function call stack, undo operations.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of push and pop operations in a Stack?
questions.hint: You only ever touch the top of the stack.
questions.solution: O(1) — Both push and pop operate on the top of the stack, so no traversal is needed.

questions.question: Which principle does a Queue follow?
questions.hint: Think of a line at a ticket counter — who gets served first?
questions.solution: FIFO (First In, First Out) — The first element added is the first one removed.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at the head of a Linked List?
questions.hint: You only need to update a few pointers.
questions.solution: O(1) — Inserting at the head only requires updating the new node's next pointer and the head pointer.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of finding the middle element of a singly linked list?
questions.hint: Think about the two-pointer (slow & fast) technique.
questions.solution: O(n) — You must traverse at least half the list. Using slow & fast pointers: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. When fast reaches end, slow is at the middle.

questions.question: What is the prerequisite for Binary Search to work?
questions.hint: Binary Search divides the search space in half each time.
questions.solution: The array must be sorted. Binary Search compares the target with the middle element and eliminates half the search space.
options: Array must be in descending order

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Binary Search?
questions.hint: Each step halves the remaining search space.
questions.solution: O(log n) — At each step, the search space is halved. So for n elements, at most log₂(n) steps are needed.

questions.question: What traversal of a BST gives elements in sorted order?
questions.hint: Think: left subtree, root, right subtree.
questions.solution: Inorder traversal (Left → Root → Right) of a BST gives elements in sorted ascending order.

questions.question: What is the height of a balanced binary tree with n nodes?
questions.hint: At each level, the number of nodes can double.
questions.solution: O(log n) — In a balanced binary tree, each level doubles the nodes. So the height is approximately log₂(n).

questions.question: Which algorithm is used for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
questions.hint: It explores nodes level by level.
questions.solution: BFS (Breadth-First Search) — It explores all neighbors at the present depth before going deeper, finding shortest path (in terms of edges) in unweighted graphs.

questions.question: What is memoization in Dynamic Programming?
questions.hint: It avoids recomputing results you've already calculated.
questions.solution: Memoization is top-down DP — store results of expensive function calls and return the cached result when the same inputs occur again. It uses recursion + a cache (usually a hashmap or array).

questions.question: What is the best-case time complexity of Bubble Sort?
questions.hint: What happens when the array is already sorted?
questions.solution: O(n) — With the optimization of tracking swaps, if no swap occurs in a pass, the array is already sorted and we stop early.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm has the best average-case time complexity among the comparison-based sorts listed?
questions.hint: This algorithm uses a pivot element to partition the array.
questions.solution: Quick Sort — Average case: O(n log n). It uses divide-and-conquer with a pivot. Best when pivot divides array evenly.

questions.question: What is the average-case time complexity for lookup in a Hash Table?
questions.hint: Hash tables are designed for fast retrieval.
questions.solution: O(1) — The hash function maps a key directly to an index, so lookup is nearly instantaneous on average (collision-free).

questions.question: What is the space complexity of a recursive solution that makes n recursive calls?
questions.hint: Each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack.
questions.solution: O(n) — Each recursive call is added to the call stack. For n recursive calls, the stack holds up to n frames simultaneously.

questions.question: What technique is best suited to find the maximum sum subarray of size k?
questions.hint: Avoid recalculating the sum from scratch for each window.
questions.solution: Sliding Window — Compute the sum for the first window, then slide: subtract the outgoing element and add the incoming element. Time: O(n).

questions.question: What is the time complexity of finding a pair with a given sum in a sorted array using two pointers?
questions.hint: One pointer starts at the left, another at the right.
questions.solution: O(n) — Two pointers start from both ends. If sum is too large, move right pointer left. If too small, move left pointer right. Each pointer moves at most n times.

questions.question: Which of the following problems can NOT be solved optimally with a greedy approach?
questions.hint: One of these requires considering all possible combinations.
questions.solution: 0/1 Knapsack — Unlike Fractional Knapsack, you cannot take fractions of items. The greedy choice (highest value/weight ratio) doesn't always yield the optimal answer. DP is required.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of generating all permutations of a string of length n?
questions.hint: How many permutations of n distinct characters are there?
questions.solution: O(n × n!) — There are n! permutations, and each takes O(n) time to construct/print. Backtracking explores all branches of this recursion tree.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the Two Sum problem using a HashMap?
questions.hint: You can check for the complement in a single pass.
questions.solution: O(n) — Iterate through the array once. For each element, check if its complement (target - element) exists in the HashMap. Insert each element into the map.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap?
questions.hint: Each vertex and each edge is processed with heap operations.
questions.solution: O((V + E) log V) — Using a min-heap: each vertex is inserted/extracted once (O(V log V)), and each edge triggers a decrease-key operation (O(E log V)).

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of searching in an unbalanced BST?
questions.hint: What happens if every node has only one child?
questions.solution: O(n) — In the worst case, the BST degenerates into a linked list (e.g., inserting sorted elements). Searching requires traversing all n nodes.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an array of n elements?
questions.hint: It's more efficient than inserting elements one by one.
questions.solution: O(n) — Using heapify bottom-up (Floyd's algorithm) builds the heap in linear time. This is better than O(n log n) for n individual insertions.
